other_observers: Paul Differding Charlie Lawrence Randy Lentz Glenn Walbek; Randy Lentz was the finder of the bird.
duration: four times for a few seconds each
specific_location: Star K Ranch (1602 E. Smith Road) Located in northern Aurora off of Smith Road on Laredo, in between Chambers Road and Airport Boulevard, south of Interstate 70 and north of Colfax.  The Woodcock was along the south side of the creek along the wooded and brushy hillside below and to the east of the orange construction fencing on the top of the south bank.
description: Medium sized brown bird a little larger than the Wilson's Snipe we flush about 7 minutes prior to seeing the woodcock. Bird was plump or "chunky" as it flew. Bill was long and straight. Wings relatively short and slightly rounded (not severely pointed like most other shorebirds). Head, back and tail from the top were medium to dark brown. The undersides were a light reddish-buff we all noted this as we had just seen a Wilson's Snipe and all immediately called it correctly based upon seeing the white belly as it flew past us.Behaviors: We initially flushed the woodcock unknowingly and only knew it once we heard the twittering of its wings (I did not personally see the bird at that time, however Randy Lentz did as he was up high on the hillside). We regrouped and spread out to methodically go through the area the bird was see to land in. As we go close (approximately 6-10yards) the bird took off, flew up high over the hill side and nearly buzzed Randy as it flew east). We noted the birds likely position (could never see the actual spot the bird came down in due to the trees and underbrush blocking our view) and proceded to slowly search for it again. We flush it twice more (never getting to see it on the ground, typical!) and the last time I flushed it from a dense twiggy thicket and it flew out and over Glenn, Paul and Charlie who all got great looks at its undersides! The bird was looked for once more but could not be refound. Each time the bird was flushed it flew from some pretty dense scrubby cover (not from the leaf litter out in the open). And each time the bird flushed we could hear the wing "twitter" as it flushed. The "twitter" either stopped on its own or we could no longer hear it because we were out of range a short time after seeing the bird pass by. Basically, after the bird past us by, we stopped hearing the wing "twitter".Call: No song or call heard. Wing "twitter" heard four times (the number of times the bird flushed). Wing "twitter" was of a short duration (either because it stopped as the bird flew or perhaps we became "out of range" and were not able to hear the sound).Plumage: Basic
